meta.title: AI Architecture Practitioner
meta.subtitle: From first principles to secure local deployment and IT handoff

sections.title: Objective
items: Create a repeatable learning environment and establish an honest baseline.

sections.title: Install or obtain access to a Linux environment
items: Native Linux, a dedicated Linux workstation, a Linux virtual machine, or WSL2.

sections.title: Complete four diagnostic tasks
items: Convert decimal numbers to binary and hexadecimal.
Multiply two small matrices by hand.
Write a Python program that reads a text file and counts words.
Explain what happens after a browser sends an HTTP request.

sections.title: Write a two-page baseline explanation titled
items: What Happens After a User Presses Send in a Local AI Application?
Do not research it first. This document becomes the baseline against which you measure later mastery.

sections.title: Exit gate
items: You can rebuild the environment from written instructions and clone, run, and modify a small Python project.

modules.title: Mathematics for AI Systems
overview: You do not need to become a pure mathematician. You need enough mathematical fluency to see what the software is doing rather than treating tensors, gradients, and probabilities as incantations.

Khan Academy is the main mathematics anchor. Use its Linear Algebra, Statistics and Probability, and selected Multivariable Calculus material. Use 3Blue1Brown alongside it for visual understanding of vectors, transformations, calculus, and neural networks. (Khan Academy)

sections.title: Build
items: A Python notebook plotting linear, polynomial, exponential, and logarithmic functions.
A table showing how logarithms relate multiplication to addition.
A simple explanation of why log probabilities are useful when multiplying many small probabilities.

sections.title: Exit question
items: Why do exponential and logarithmic functions appear in softmax, loss calculations, probability, and model scaling?

sections.title: Study
items: Broadcasting as a later programming implementation

sections.title: def transpose(matrix)
items: Then compare your implementation against NumPy.

sections.title: Create a shape ledger for every operation
Exit gate
items: Given two tensor shapes, you can determine whether matrix multiplication is valid and predict the output shape.

sections.title: Study
items: Eigenvectors and eigenvalues at a conceptual level
3Blue1Brown’s material on vectors, linear transformations, and dot products is particularly useful here. (3Blue1Brown)

sections.title: Build
items: Examples of rotation, scaling, shear, and projection.
A diagram explaining how vector similarity can support semantic search without claiming that the vector itself “understands” the document.

sections.title: Exit gate
items: You can explain why a learned linear layer is more than a spreadsheet of arbitrary numbers.

weeks.title: Derivatives, chain rule, partial derivatives, and gradients

sections.title: Study
items: Khan Academy’s multivariable material includes partial derivatives and gradient concepts suitable for this foundation. (Khan Academy)

sections.title: Build
items: Gradient descent for a one-variable function.
Gradient descent for a two-variable function.
A visual trace showing how different learning rates converge, oscillate, or diverge.

sections.title: Exit gate
items: You can explain the chain rule and how an error signal propagates backward through a sequence of operations.

closingSections.title: Module deliverables
items: Five-minute client explanation: How a model turns scores into a token choice

closingSections.title: You can
items: Calculate a dot product and matrix product.
Explain gradients without relying only on a downhill metaphor.
Show why softmax does not make a model’s answer factually correct.

modules.title: Python, Software Engineering, Git, and SQL
overview: Use Harvard’s free CS50 Python course for Python fundamentals, selected CS50x material for computer-science foundations, CS50 SQL for relational data, and GitHub Skills for practical Git workflows. CS50P covers functions, exceptions, testing, files, regular expressions, object-oriented programming, and related Python foundations. (edX)

sections.title: Build
items: A structured logger that writes timestamped events.

sections.title: A document-ingestion command that
items: Logs errors without crashing the entire batch

sections.title: Introduce
items: Document how each failure is detected and reported.

sections.title: Build
items: Convert the ingestion project into a structured package.
Introduce a defect on a branch, diagnose it, and revert or repair it.

sections.title: Exit question
items: What is the difference between code version, configuration version, data version, and model version?

sections.title: A SQLite or PostgreSQL database containing
items: A small Python service that can create, retrieve, update, and list records.

closingSections.title: Module deliverables
items: Client explanation: Why an AI application still needs ordinary software engineering

closingSections.title: Module exit gate
items: You can trace data through functions, tests, files, and database records, and can identify whether a defect belongs to the code, configuration, data, or environment.

modules.title: Digital Logic, CPU, Memory, and Data Representation
overview: Use Nand2Tetris to see how logic gates, arithmetic, memory, machine language, and a computer architecture fit together. Its materials and browser-based development environment allow you to construct much of the stack without physical electronics. (nand2tetris)

sections.title: Build
items: File inspector showing the raw bytes behind a short text file.

sections.title: Exit gate
items: You can explain why “four-bit model weights” do not mean that every part of the running system uses four-bit numbers.

sections.title: Build selected Nand2Tetris projects
items: Do not attempt to memorize gate diagrams. Concentrate on how layers of abstraction are composed.

sections.title: A diagram showing
items: Disk → system RAM → accelerator memory → compute units

sections.title: A model-memory estimator
items: weight memory ≈ parameter count × bits per parameter ÷ 8

closingSections.title: You can accurately distinguish
items: Compute throughput from end-to-end application speed
Model weight memory from total runtime memory

modules.title: Linux, Processes, Networking, Storage, and Virtualization
overview: Use the Linux Foundation’s free Introduction to Linux course, MIT’s Missing Semester, Cisco’s Networking Basics material, and MDN’s HTTP documentation. These cover the command line, processes, practical development tools, networking foundations, and request-response behavior. (Linux Foundation - Education)

sections.title: Build
items: A Python process that runs under that account.
A script that records CPU, memory, and disk use.

sections.title: Build
items: A system service for the earlier Python API.

weeks.title: Networking, DNS, TCP, HTTP, and TLS

sections.title: Build a two-host or two-VM lab
items: Client VM → reverse proxy → application VM

sections.title: Build
items: Backup procedure for application data and configuration.
Evidence that the restored service works.
Written record of elapsed recovery steps—not merely “backup completed.”

closingSections.title: Phase I exit examination
items: Starting with a file on disk, explain how it becomes bytes in memory, is processed by a running service, sent through a network socket, and returned as an HTTP response.

modules.title: Containers, Automation, and Orchestration
overview: Use Docker’s official hands-on introductory material, Ansible’s documentation, and Kubernetes Basics. Docker’s training covers images, containers, volumes, networking, and Compose; Ansible playbooks provide repeatable machine configuration; Kubernetes Basics introduces deployment, scaling, updating, and debugging of containerized applications. (Docker)

sections.title: Build
items: Run it without root inside the container.

sections.title: Exit question
items: Which parts of the system are inside the container, which remain on the host, and which persist after the container is deleted?

sections.title: Build a Compose stack containing
items: Secrets supplied separately from the image

sections.title: Use Ansible to
items: Install or update the container stack.
Complete the Kubernetes Basics material, but do not automatically move the capstone to Kubernetes.

sections.title: Write a decision record answering
items: Is this a single-host or multi-host service?
How many services and replicas exist?
Does the client have Kubernetes competence?
Who will patch and troubleshoot the cluster?
Is the added operational burden justified?

My recommendation is direct: master Linux, containers, Compose, and repeatable configuration before adopting Kubernetes. Kubernetes should answer a real operating requirement rather than serve as a mark of sophistication.

closingSections.title: Module exit gate
items: A clean Linux host can be configured and brought to a working application state from your repository and runbook.

modules.title: Machine-Learning Concepts and Evaluation
overview: Use Google’s Machine Learning Crash Course, including its material on fairness and production machine-learning systems. It provides a practical introduction to model training, metrics, generalization, data, and production concerns. (Google for Developers)

sections.title: Build
items: A small classification or regression dataset.

sections.title: Study
items: Use a practical scenario where the costs are asymmetric. For example, a triage system may impose different harm when it misses a high-risk case than when it flags a low-risk case for human review.
This does not mean maximizing recall blindly. It means explicitly connecting metrics to consequences, staffing, workflow, and affected populations.

closingSections.title: Module deliverables
items: Client explanation: Why model accuracy is not sufficient

closingSections.title: Module exit gate
items: You can choose a metric based on operational consequences and identify overfitting, leakage, and subgroup failures.

modules.title: Neural Networks and PyTorch
overview: Use PyTorch’s Learn the Basics material, fast.ai’s Practical Deep Learning course, and the free interactive book Dive into Deep Learning. Together they cover tensors, automatic differentiation, training loops, model construction, and practical experimentation. (PyTorch Documentation)

sections.title: Overview
items: Before relying on PyTorch autograd, implement a tiny scalar differentiation engine.

sections.title: A scalar should record
items: Use it to compute and differentiate a small expression.

sections.title: Then reproduce the same computation in PyTorch and inspect
Exit gate
items: You can distinguish values, operations, gradients, and parameters.

weeks.title: Tensors, layers, activations, and forward passes

sections.title: Build
items: A multilayer perceptron from explicit tensor operations.
The same model using PyTorch modules.
A shape trace for every layer.

sections.title: Intentionally introduce
items: Create a troubleshooting guide based on the symptoms.

sections.title: Overview
items: Train a small network on an appropriate public dataset.

sections.title: Record
items: Energy estimate or observed system power where feasible

closingSections.title: You can explain and demonstrate
items: Why a checkpoint is not the same thing as source code

modules.title: Transformers and Language-Model Internals
overview: Use the Hugging Face learning materials, Harvard’s Annotated Transformer, and selected Stanford CS336 material. The Annotated Transformer walks through the original architecture in executable form, while CS336 treats language modeling as a system built from data, tokenization, model components, training, scaling, and evaluation. (Hugging Face)

sections.title: Study
items: Hugging Face’s tokenizer documentation provides direct access to tokenizer behavior and training concepts. (Hugging Face)

sections.title: Build
items: Train a small tokenizer on a public-domain corpus.

sections.title: Inspect how it tokenizes
items: Indigenous place names, where appropriate source material exists
Compare token counts between two tokenizers.

sections.title: Client explanation
items: Why a “word” and a “token” are not equivalent, and why token counts affect context and operating cost.

sections.title: Build
items: Demonstration that removing positional information changes the system’s ability to distinguish order.

sections.title: Study
items: Build single-head attention first, then multi-head attention.
Do not copy an entire implementation. Write and test each operation separately.

sections.title: Build
items: Basic cache demonstration or instrumentation showing why reusing prior key/value states reduces repeated computation.

closingSections.title: Module deliverables
items: Unit tests for attention and masking

closingSections.title: Starting with a sentence, you can trace
items: You can also distinguish architecture, configuration, tokenizer, weights, context, and generated output.

modules.title: Training a Language Model From Random Initialization
overview: Use selected Stanford CS336 assignments and PyTorch’s distributed documentation. CS336 includes work on data filtering, deduplication, training, scaling, and distributed systems; PyTorch documents data parallelism, fully sharded training, tensor parallelism, and pipeline approaches. (Stanford CS336)

sections.title: Build a small public-domain or synthetic corpus pipeline
items: Produce a manifest with hashes and source information.

sections.title: Overview
items: Train your decoder-only Transformer from random initialization.
Keep it small enough to permit repeated experiments, perhaps roughly one to twenty million parameters depending on available hardware.

sections.title: Practice
items: The goal is not a useful foundation model. The goal is to see every moving part.

sections.title: Study
items: Complete a two-process experiment on CPU or compatible accelerators where possible. The model can be tiny.

closingSections.title: Module exit gate
items: You can explain precisely what “trained from scratch” means and why training memory is significantly larger than raw weight storage.

modules.title: Model Packages, Adaptation, Fine-Tuning, Quantization, and GPU Execution
overview: Use Hugging Face’s free post-training course, its TRL documentation, its quantization material, and NVIDIA’s current CUDA programming guide for accelerator concepts. The post-training ecosystem covers supervised fine-tuning and preference-oriented methods; the quantization material explains reduced-precision model representation; the CUDA guide explains the execution and memory model beneath GPU workloads. (Hugging Face)

sections.title: Overview
items: Inspect a real open model package.

sections.title: Exit gate
items: You can explain why copying “the model file” is not always sufficient to reproduce a deployment.

sections.title: Evaluate each against
items: Is the problem knowledge or behavior?

sections.title: A central rule
items: Use the least invasive intervention that solves the measured problem.

sections.title: Overview
items: Complete a small supervised fine-tuning or LoRA exercise.

sections.title: Compare
items: Use a fixed evaluation set. Do not judge improvement from a few attractive examples.

sections.title: Study
items: FP32, FP16, BF16, FP8, INT8, and low-bit formats
Build a benchmark comparing at least two numerical representations or quantization levels:

closingSections.title: Given a model card and a client requirement, you can defend
items: Why a particular tokenizer and format are required
Whether retrieval or fine-tuning is appropriate
What memory and hardware are required
How the system will be evaluated before deployment

modules.title: Local Inference and Model Serving
overview: Start with llama.cpp, which supports local execution and an HTTP server across a range of hardware. Then use vLLM for GPU-oriented serving and an OpenAI-compatible service interface. (GitHub)
I recommend learning llama.cpp before a higher-level desktop manager because it exposes model formats, context limits, threads, device offloading, server arguments, and memory behavior more plainly.

sections.title: Practice
items: Partial or full accelerator offload where supported
Build a system-service definition for the model server.

sections.title: Where a compatible accelerator is available, practice
items: Free notebook services can assist with limited experiments, but free compute resources are not guaranteed and should never be treated as production capacity. (Google Research)

closingSections.title: Module exit gate
items: You can explain why a model may load successfully but fail under real concurrency, and why throughput and latency are not interchangeable.

modules.title: APIs, Data Engineering, Retrieval, and RAG
overview: Use FastAPI’s official tutorial for application service construction, CS50 SQL for relational data, Qdrant’s free Essentials course for vector and hybrid retrieval concepts, and Sentence Transformers for embeddings and retrieve-then-rerank patterns. (FastAPI)
Build this module without a large agent or RAG framework. The abstraction should be yours before a framework conceals it.

sections.title: Metadata should include
items: Test updates and deletions, not only initial insertion.

sections.title: Build
items: A small labeled question-to-document test set
Measure whether the expected source appears in the retrieved results.

weeks.title: Keyword search, hybrid retrieval, reranking, and permissions

sections.title: Compare
items: Add permission filtering before context reaches the model.

sections.title: Attempt to retrieve a restricted document through
items: The test passes only when authorization is enforced independently of the model.

sections.title: Create a failure taxonomy
items: Relevant source exists but was not indexed
It was indexed but not retrieved
It was retrieved but ranked too low
It was supplied but the model ignored it
Citation points to the wrong passage

closingSections.title: Module exit gate
items: You can distinguish retrieval failure from generation failure and prove that restricted sources cannot reach unauthorized users.

modules.title: Tools, Workflows, Agents, and MCP
overview: Use Hugging Face’s free Agents course for tool and agent concepts, followed by the official Model Context Protocol architecture and security guidance. (Hugging Face)

sections.title: Build a read-only tool such as
The model may propose a tool call. The application must
Then a separate deterministic approval path
items: The model must not possess unrestricted authority to perform the final action.

sections.title: Implement or inspect a simple MCP server and map its trust boundaries

closingSections.title: You can demonstrate that
items: Model output is treated as a proposal.
Authorization is enforced by ordinary software.
A malicious document cannot silently grant tool privileges.
Duplicate calls do not create duplicate actions.
An administrator can disable the tool without disabling the whole model.

modules.title: Security, Privacy, and AI Governance
overview: Use the OWASP 2025 LLM and GenAI security material, OWASP’s 2026 agentic security work, MITRE ATLAS, and the NIST AI Risk Management Framework and Generative AI Profile. OWASP and MITRE catalog practical attack patterns, while NIST organizes risk work around Govern, Map, Measure, and Manage. (OWASP Gen AI Security Project)

sections.title: Failure lab
items: Replace a model or container artifact with a modified file and show how integrity verification detects the change.

sections.title: Test whether sensitive information appears in
items: Local hosting reduces some external data exposure, but it does not remove internal privacy risk.

sections.title: Conduct an incident tabletop
items: A malicious uploaded document influenced the assistant to request an unauthorized tool action, and sensitive content appeared in telemetry.

closingSections.title: Module exit gate
items: You can map concrete controls to concrete risks. “It runs locally” and “the model was instructed not to do that” are not accepted security controls.

modules.title: Observability, Evaluation, Reliability, and Energy
overview: Use OpenTelemetry for traces, metrics, and logs; Prometheus and Grafana for collection and dashboards; evaluation tools such as Ragas, Hugging Face Evaluate, or MLflow; and the Software Carbon Intensity specification for disciplined energy and carbon accounting. (OpenTelemetry)
Google’s freely available Site Reliability Engineering books are valuable supplementary reading for service objectives, monitoring, incident management, and sustainable operations. (Google SRE)

sections.title: Record suitable metadata
items: Do not place unrestricted prompt and document content into general telemetry.

sections.title: Build dashboards for
items: Define service objectives based on the use case, not arbitrary industry numbers.

sections.title: Create a versioned evaluation suite covering
Run the suite whenever any of these change
Measure, where equipment allows
items: Energy per fixed number of generated tokens
Utilization during peak and off-peak periods
Record assumptions where direct measurement is impossible.
The environmental goal is not merely to purchase efficient hardware. It is to avoid unnecessary computation through:
Hardware consolidation without dangerous single points of failure

closingSections.title: Phase III exit examination
items: Given a failed user request, trace it through identity, retrieval, model execution, tools, and telemetry; identify the failed layer; apply a repair; run regression evaluations; and produce an incident record.

modules.title: Discovery, Architecture, Deployment, and Knowledge Transfer
overview: This final module converts technical competence into client competence.

sections.title: Business and workflow
items: What decision or task is being supported?
What is slow, expensive, inconsistent, or inaccessible?
What must remain a human decision?
What happens when the system is wrong?
What is the fallback during an outage?

sections.title: Users and access
items: Are there elderly users, people with disabilities, field workers, low-bandwidth locations, or users operating under stress?
Which languages and reading levels must be supported?

sections.title: Data
items: What retention and deletion obligations exist?
Are there protected, confidential, personnel, medical, legal, or tribal data concerns?

sections.title: Operations
items: Existing identity, logging, and monitoring systems
Power, cooling, rack, and network capacity

sections.title: Governance
items: Produce a formal use-case and non-use-case statement.

sections.title: Explain each component in three forms
Overview
items: Deploy the capstone onto a clean target environment.

sections.title: The final system must include
items: At least one approval-gated synthetic action

sections.title: Help-desk or user-support staff
items: Distinguish outage, retrieval, permission, and answer-quality problems

sections.title: Run four table exercises
items: Users can log in but cannot retrieve documents.
A new model is faster but fails important evaluation cases.
Sensitive content appears in an application trace.
The trainees—not you—must operate the system.

sections.title: Overview
items: Deliver the final system and defend it before a mock review panel.

sections.title: The panel should ask
items: How many users can it support?
What happens when VRAM is exhausted?
How do you detect unsupported answers?
Can a retrieved document command the system?
How is a model upgrade evaluated?
What would make you advise against deployment?

sections.title: Your final client package should contain
items: Hardware and software bill of materials
Model, tokenizer, adapter, and data inventory

sections.title: A new administrator must be able to
items: Identify the model and data versions.
You may observe, but you should not rescue the administrator at the first difficulty. Any point where the administrator becomes dependent on undocumented knowledge is a handoff defect.

groups.title: What “intimate knowledge” means
items: You do not need to fabricate a semiconductor or rewrite a vendor’s matrix multiplication library. You do need to be able to:
Trace a request from the user interface down to the accelerator and back.
Calculate approximate model, context, and concurrency memory requirements.
Explain what tokenization, embeddings, attention, weights, inference, retrieval, and tool use actually do.
Implement a small neural network and a tiny Transformer rather than only importing one.
Train a small language model from random initialization.
Package, quantize, serve, benchmark, and monitor an existing model.
Build retrieval without hiding behind a large orchestration framework.
Enforce permissions independently of the model.
Prove whether the system works with evaluations rather than demonstrations alone.
Produce deployment, rollback, backup, restoration, security, and incident runbooks.
Train the client’s IT personnel until they can run the system without you.

groups.title: Overview
items: Every module produces five forms of evidence:

groups.title: AI-assistant rule
items: Use AI assistance for explanations, hints, code review, test generation, and error interpretation. Do not accept code you cannot explain.
Rebuild the central portion from a blank file.
State what enters and leaves every service.
Identify where credentials and sensitive data appear.
Predict at least three failure modes.
The objective is not to avoid AI assistance. It is to prevent assistance from concealing a knowledge gap.

groups.title: Overview
items: You will build one system throughout the program:

groups.title: Local Knowledge and Operations Platform
items: change control | energy measurement | IT runbooks
Use public-domain, openly licensed, or synthetic documents during development. Do not use real protected client records while learning.
A suitable synthetic scenario would be an organizational assistant that can:
Answer questions from policies, plans, schedules, and standard operating procedures.
Look up synthetic project or work-order records.
Draft—but not independently authorize—a work order.
Escalate uncertain or high-consequence questions to a human.
Maintain an auditable record of system actions.
It should never be presented as the sole source for evacuation orders, emergency medical decisions, incident command, or other life-safety decisions.

groups.title: Repository structure
items: Create this in Week 0 and maintain it through Week 60:

groups.title: Examination A — Week 19: Packet to Process
items: You pass when you can diagnose failures at each boundary.

groups.title: Examination B — Week 38: Text to Weights to Text
items: You pass when you can build and train a tiny model rather than only describe one.

groups.title: Examination C — Week 55: Request to Audited Response
items: You pass when you can distinguish a model failure from a retrieval, permission, tool, application, or infrastructure failure.

groups.title: Examination D — Week 60: Independent Client Ownership
items: You pass when another person can safely operate the stack from your documentation.

groups.title: Overview
items: The following are automatic non-passes regardless of total score:
A model or data change is deployed without evaluation.
Credentials are embedded in source code or images.
The student cannot explain the code responsible for a security boundary.
A high-consequence system lacks a human fallback.
The system is represented as authoritative beyond its tested scope.

groups.title: Mathematics
items: Khan Academy Linear Algebra, Statistics and Probability, and selected Multivariable Calculus. (Khan Academy)

groups.title: Linux, networking, and deployment
items: Linux Foundation Introduction to Linux. (Linux Foundation - Education)

groups.title: Machine learning and neural networks
items: Google Machine Learning Crash Course. (Google for Developers)
fast.ai Practical Deep Learning. (Practical Deep Learning for Coders)
Dive into Deep Learning. (Dive into Deep Learning)

groups.title: Adaptation and performance
items: Hugging Face post-training course and TRL. (Hugging Face)

groups.title: Agents and tools
items: Model Context Protocol documentation. (Model Context Protocol)

groups.title: Security, governance, and operations
items: OWASP LLM and GenAI Security. (OWASP Gen AI Security Project)
OWASP Agentic Security. (OWASP Gen AI Security Project)
NIST AI RMF and Generative AI Profile. (NIST)
Software Carbon Intensity specification. (Green Software Foundation)

groups.title: Overview
items: After the core program, the following electives provide deeper specialization.

groups.title: A. GPU kernels and performance engineering — 4 to 6 weeks
items: Performance comparison against ordinary CPU implementation
This is appropriate when you need to diagnose runtime behavior below the inference-engine layer.

groups.title: B. Distributed and high-availability AI systems — 4 weeks
C. Advanced AI security — 4 weeks
items: Produce evidence that every major control is enforced outside the language model.

groups.title: D. Multimodal and field systems — 4 to 6 weeks
items: This becomes particularly relevant for field operations, land management, emergency support, and rural service delivery.

groups.title: Overview
items: Create the curriculum repository and directory structure.
Record your hardware, operating system, Python, and Git environment.
Write the baseline explanation of what happens after a user presses Send.
Draw the first version of the full stack from physical hardware through governance.

Do not try to make the first explanation impressive. Make it honest. At Week 60, rewrite the same document. The difference between those two explanations will show, more clearly than any certificate, how much of the architecture you have actually mastered.
